From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Saint regarded as a heavenly advocate

For the racehorse, see [Patron Saint (horse)](/wiki/Patron_Saint_(horse) "Patron Saint (horse)").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/53/Nicolas_R%C3%A9gnier_-_Saint_Matthew_and_the_Angel.jpg/250px-Nicolas_R%C3%A9gnier_-_Saint_Matthew_and_the_Angel.jpg)](/wiki/File:Nicolas_R%C3%A9gnier_-_Saint_Matthew_and_the_Angel.jpg)

[Saint Matthew the Apostle](/wiki/Matthew_the_Apostle "Matthew the Apostle"), depicted by [Nicolas Régnier](/wiki/Nicolas_R%C3%A9gnier "Nicolas Régnier"), is the patron saint of [Salerno](/wiki/Salerno "Salerno"), Italy, bankers, and tax collectors.

A **patron saint**, **patroness saint**, **patron hallow** or **heavenly protector** is a [saint](/wiki/Saint "Saint") who in [Catholicism](/wiki/Catholic_Church "Catholic Church"), [Anglicanism](/wiki/Anglicanism "Anglicanism"), [Eastern Orthodoxy](/wiki/Eastern_Orthodoxy "Eastern Orthodoxy") or [Oriental Orthodoxy](/wiki/Oriental_Orthodoxy "Oriental Orthodoxy") is regarded as the heavenly [advocate](/wiki/Advocacy "Advocacy") of a nation, place, craft, activity, class, clan, family, or person.[[1]](#cite_note-1)[[2]](#cite_note-2)

The term may be applied to individuals to whom similar roles are ascribed in other [religions](/wiki/Religion "Religion").

## In Christianity

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Patron_saint&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: In Christianity")]

Saints often become the patrons of places where they were born or had been active. However, there were cases in [medieval Europe](/wiki/Middle_Ages "Middle Ages") where a city which grew to prominence obtained for its cathedral the remains or some [relics](/wiki/Relic "Relic") of a famous saint who had lived and was buried elsewhere, thus making them the city's patron saint – such a practice conferred considerable prestige on the city concerned. In [Latin America](/wiki/Latin_America "Latin America") and the [Philippines](/wiki/Philippines "Philippines"), Spanish and Portuguese explorers often named a location for the saint on whose [feast](/wiki/Feast_day "Feast day") or commemoration day they first visited the place, with that saint naturally becoming the area's patron.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/60/Saint_Honore_Cake_Shop_Outlet01.jpg/250px-Saint_Honore_Cake_Shop_Outlet01.jpg)](/wiki/File:Saint_Honore_Cake_Shop_Outlet01.jpg)

A branch of [Saint Honore Cake Shop](/wiki/Saint_Honore_Cake_Shop "Saint Honore Cake Shop"), a [Hong Kong](/wiki/Hong_Kong "Hong Kong") chain bakery. [Honoratus of Amiens](/wiki/Honoratus_of_Amiens "Honoratus of Amiens") is the patron saint of bakers and confectioners.

Occupations sometimes have a patron saint who had been connected somewhat with it, although some of the connections were tenuous. Lacking such a saint, an occupation would have a patron whose acts or miracles in some way recall the profession. For example, when the previously unknown occupation of [photography](/wiki/Photography "Photography") appeared in the 19th century, [Saint Veronica](/wiki/Saint_Veronica "Saint Veronica") was made its patron, owing to how [her veil](/wiki/Veil_of_Veronica "Veil of Veronica") miraculously received the imprint of [Christ](/wiki/Jesus "Jesus")'s [face](/wiki/Holy_Face_of_Jesus "Holy Face of Jesus") after she wiped off the blood and sweat.[[3]](#cite_note-3)[[4]](#cite_note-4)[[5]](#cite_note-5).

The [veneration](/wiki/Veneration "Veneration") or commemoration and recognition of patron saints or saints in general is found in [Catholicism](/wiki/Catholic_Church "Catholic Church") (including [Eastern Catholicism](/wiki/Eastern_Catholic_Churches "Eastern Catholic Churches")), [Eastern Orthodoxy](/wiki/Eastern_Orthodoxy "Eastern Orthodoxy"), [Oriental Orthodoxy](/wiki/Oriental_Orthodox_Churches "Oriental Orthodox Churches"), and among some [Lutherans](/wiki/Lutheranism "Lutheranism") and [Anglicans](/wiki/Anglicanism "Anglicanism").[[6]](#cite_note-Brandsrud2022-6) According to the [Catholic catechism](/wiki/Catechism_of_the_Catholic_Church "Catechism of the Catholic Church") a [person's](/wiki/Faithful_(Catholicism) "Faithful (Catholicism)") patron saint, having already attained the [beatific vision](/wiki/Beatific_vision "Beatific vision"), is able to intercede with God for their needs.[[7]](#cite_note-7)

Apart from Lutheranism and Anglicanism, it is, however, generally discouraged in other [Protestant](/wiki/Protestantism "Protestantism") branches, such as [Reformed Christianity](/wiki/Calvinism "Calvinism"), where the practice is considered a form of [idolatry](/wiki/Idolatry "Idolatry").[[8]](#cite_note-8)

### Catholicism

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Patron_saint&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Catholicism")]

A saint can be assigned as a patron by a venerable tradition, or chosen by election. The saint is considered a special intercessor with God and the proper advocate of a particular locality, occupation, etc., and merits a special form of religious observance. A term in some ways comparable is "titular", which is applicable only to a church or institution.[[9]](#cite_note-9)

## In Islam

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Patron_saint&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: In Islam")]

Although Islam has no codified doctrine of patronage on the part of saints, it has nevertheless been an important part of both [Sunni](/wiki/Sunni_Islam "Sunni Islam") and [Shia](/wiki/Shia_Islam "Shia Islam") Islamic traditions that particularly important classical saints have served as the heavenly advocates for specific Muslim [empires](/wiki/Empire "Empire"), [nations](/wiki/Nation "Nation"), [cities](/wiki/City "City"), [towns](/wiki/Town "Town"), and [villages](/wiki/Village "Village").[[10]](#cite_note-Lings-10) [Martin Lings](/wiki/Martin_Lings "Martin Lings") wrote: "There is scarcely a region in the empire of Islam which has not a Sufi for its Patron Saint."[[10]](#cite_note-Lings-10): 119 As the veneration accorded saints often develops purely organically in Islamic climates,[*[neutrality](/wiki/Wikipedia:Neutral_point_of_view "Wikipedia:Neutral point of view") is [disputed](/wiki/Talk:Patron_saint "Talk:Patron saint")*] in a manner different from Catholic and Eastern Orthodox [Christianity](/wiki/Christianity "Christianity"), "patron saints" are often recognized through popular acclaim rather than through official declaration.[[10]](#cite_note-Lings-10) Traditionally, it has been understood that the patron saint of a particular place prays for that place's wellbeing and for the health and happiness of all who live therein.[[10]](#cite_note-Lings-10)

However, the [Wahhabi](/wiki/Wahhabism "Wahhabism") and [Salafi](/wiki/Salafi_movement "Salafi movement") movements have latterly attacked the veneration of saints (as patron or otherwise), which they claim are a form of idolatry or *[shirk](/wiki/Shirk_(Islam) "Shirk (Islam)")*.[[10]](#cite_note-Lings-10) More mainstream Sunni clerics have critiqued this argument since Wahhabism first emerged in the 18th century.[[11]](#cite_note-11)

## In Druze faith

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Patron_saint&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: In Druze faith")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3f/Malby-Hejn%C3%A1k-Sv.Ji%C5%99%C3%AD.jpg/250px-Malby-Hejn%C3%A1k-Sv.Ji%C5%99%C3%AD.jpg)](/wiki/File:Malby-Hejn%C3%A1k-Sv.Ji%C5%99%C3%AD.jpg)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ef/Tortosa_catedral_Huguet_Transfiguracio_Ahazi%C3%A0_0017.jpg/250px-Tortosa_catedral_Huguet_Transfiguracio_Ahazi%C3%A0_0017.jpg)](/wiki/File:Tortosa_catedral_Huguet_Transfiguracio_Ahazi%C3%A0_0017.jpg)

Patron saints of the [Druze](/wiki/Druze "Druze"):[[12]](#cite_note-Fukasawa_2017_310-12) [Saint George](/wiki/Saint_George "Saint George") (left) and Saint [Elijah](/wiki/Elijah "Elijah") (right)

[Elijah](/wiki/Elijah "Elijah") and [Jethro](/wiki/Jethro_(biblical_figure) "Jethro (biblical figure)") ([Shuaib](/wiki/Shuaib "Shuaib")) are considered patron saints of the [Druze people](/wiki/Druze_people "Druze people").[[12]](#cite_note-Fukasawa_2017_310-12)[[13]](#cite_note-13) In the [Old Testament](/wiki/Old_Testament "Old Testament"), Jethro was [Moses](/wiki/Moses "Moses")' father-in-law, a [Kenite](/wiki/Kenite "Kenite") shepherd and priest of [Midian](/wiki/Midian "Midian").[[14]](#cite_note-Harris-14) [Muslim](/wiki/Muslim "Muslim") scholars and the [Druze](/wiki/Druze "Druze") identify Jethro with the prophet Shuaib, also said to come from Midian.[[15]](#cite_note-15) Shuaib or Jethro of Midian is considered an ancestor of the Druze who revere him as their spiritual founder and chief prophet.[[16]](#cite_note-16)

Druze identify Elijah as "al-[Khidr](/wiki/Khidr "Khidr")".[[17]](#cite_note-Swayd_2015_77-17) Druze, like some [Christians](/wiki/Christians "Christians"), believe that the Prophet Elijah came back as [Saint John the Baptist](/wiki/John_the_Baptist "John the Baptist"),[[17]](#cite_note-Swayd_2015_77-17)[[18]](#cite_note-Bennett_2010_77-18) since they believe in [reincarnation](/wiki/Reincarnation "Reincarnation") and the transmigration of the soul, Druze believe that El Khidr and Saint John the Baptist are one and the same; along with [Saint George](/wiki/Saint_George "Saint George").[[18]](#cite_note-Bennett_2010_77-18)

Due to the [Christian influence on the Druze faith](/wiki/Christianity_and_Druze "Christianity and Druze"), two [Christian saints](/wiki/Christian_saints "Christian saints") become the Druze's favorite venerated figures: Saint George and Saint Elijah.[[19]](#cite_note-Beaurepaire-19) Thus, in all the villages inhabited by Druzes and Christians in central [Mount Lebanon](/wiki/Mount_Lebanon "Mount Lebanon") a Christian church or Druze maqam is dedicated to either one of them.[[19]](#cite_note-Beaurepaire-19) According to scholar Ray Jabre Mouawad the Druzes appreciated the two saints for their bravery: [Saint George because he confronted the dragon](/wiki/Saint_George_and_the_Dragon "Saint George and the Dragon") and the Prophet Elijah because he competed with and defeated the pagan priests of [Baal](/wiki/Baal "Baal").[[19]](#cite_note-Beaurepaire-19) In both cases the explanations provided by Christians is that Druzes were attracted to [warrior saints](/wiki/Military_saint "Military saint") that resemble their own militarized society.[[19]](#cite_note-Beaurepaire-19)

## In Eastern religions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Patron_saint&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: In Eastern religions")]

In [Hinduism](/wiki/Hinduism "Hinduism"), certain sects may devote themselves to the veneration of a saint, such as the [Balmiki sect](/wiki/Balmikism "Balmikism") that reveres [Valmiki](/wiki/Valmiki "Valmiki").[[20]](#cite_note-Kananaikil1983-20)

[Buddhism](/wiki/Buddhism "Buddhism") also includes the idea of protector deities, which are called "Dharma protectors" ([Dharmapala](/wiki/Dharmapala "Dharmapala")).
